title: Original Legacy Bar Multiple-Choice Drafts - Batch 003 (Internally Audited)
description: Thirty-five original, AI-assisted legacy MBE-style question drafts: five in each of the seven MBE subjects. Each item received a structured internal second-pass audit. These are not NCBE questions and are not endorsed by NCBE.

rights.marketingRestriction: Do not call these official, released, licensed, actual, or NCBE questions. Do not imply NCBE affiliation or endorsement.

styleNotes: Use a realistic factual scenario, a distinct call of the question, and four parallel options.
Test application of one principal rule, occasionally with one closely related rule.
Use only facts needed to choose the best answer; avoid trivia and local-law assumptions unless expressly stated.
Each distractor must represent a plausible legal error and receive an independent explanation.
Do not reproduce or lightly rewrite official questions or any prior proprietary question bank.

materials: User-supplied NCBE MBE sample questions, consulted only for general format and pacing.
User-supplied NCBE MBE Subject Matter Outline, used for subject and category coverage.
NCBE current MBE preparation guidance on four-option, one-best-answer items.
Current Federal Rules of Civil Procedure and Federal Rules of Evidence published by the U.S. Courts.
Current Title 28 text published by the U.S. House Office of the Law Revision Counsel where relevant.
Generally accepted common-law, UCC Article 2, and constitutional doctrines identified in the authority notes.

questions.subject: Civil Procedure
questions.subtopic: Supplemental jurisdiction - claim by plaintiff against a Rule 14 party
questions.stem: A citizen of State A sued a trucking company incorporated and having its principal place of business in State B in federal district court. The complaint sought $200,000 for injuries suffered in a highway collision and invoked only diversity jurisdiction. The trucking company filed a proper third-party complaint under Rule 14 against the truck driver, a citizen of State A, seeking indemnity. The plaintiff then asserted a $50,000 negligence claim against the driver arising from the same collision. No federal claim is involved. The driver moved to dismiss the plaintiff's claim for lack of subject-matter jurisdiction.
questions.call: Should the court grant the driver's motion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because the supplemental-jurisdiction statute bars this claim by the plaintiff against a party joined under Rule 14.
choices.rationale: When original jurisdiction rests solely on diversity, 28 U.S.C. § 1367(b) withholds supplemental jurisdiction over a claim by the original plaintiff against a person made a party under Rule 14 if the claim does not independently satisfy diversity requirements.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because no claim seeking $75,000 or less may ever fall within supplemental jurisdiction.
choices.rationale: Related claims below the diversity amount can ordinarily fall within supplemental jurisdiction. The problem here is the specific limitation in § 1367(b), not a universal amount-in-controversy rule.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because the plaintiff's claim and the indemnity claim arise from the same collision.
choices.rationale: The claims form part of the same Article III case or controversy, but § 1367(b) expressly removes supplemental jurisdiction over this type of claim by a plaintiff in a diversity-only action.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because the trucking company created jurisdiction over the driver by filing the third-party complaint.
choices.rationale: The court may have supplemental jurisdiction over the defendant's indemnity claim, but that does not supply an independent jurisdictional basis for the original plaintiff's claim against a nondiverse third-party defendant.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether supplemental jurisdiction extends to an original plaintiff's claim against a nondiverse third-party defendant in a diversity-only action.
explanation.governingRule: In an action founded solely on diversity jurisdiction, 28 U.S.C. § 1367(b) bars supplemental jurisdiction over claims by plaintiffs against persons made parties under Rule 14 when exercising jurisdiction would be inconsistent with 28 U.S.C. § 1332.
explanation.application: The driver was joined under Rule 14, is a citizen of the same state as the plaintiff, and faces a claim for only $50,000. The plaintiff's claim therefore lacks an independent diversity basis and falls within § 1367(b).
explanation.commonTrap: Stopping after finding that the claims arise from the same transaction. Section 1367(a) provides the general grant; subsection (b) then removes jurisdiction over specified plaintiff claims in diversity-only cases.
explanation.memoryAid: In diversity-only cases, watch claims by the original plaintiff against Rule 14, 19, 20, or 24 parties.

qualityAudit.secondPassNotes: Second pass clarified that the original action rests solely on diversity, specified the third-party defendant's citizenship and amount, and removed any possible federal-question basis.

questions.subject: Civil Procedure
questions.subtopic: Rule 12 defenses - waiver by omission from the first Rule 12 motion
questions.stem: A plaintiff properly filed a federal action against a defendant. Before answering, the defendant moved under Rule 12 to dismiss for failure to state a claim. The motion did not raise lack of personal jurisdiction. After the court denied that motion, but before the defendant filed an answer, the defendant filed a second Rule 12 motion seeking dismissal for lack of personal jurisdiction. The plaintiff opposed the second motion on the ground that the defense had been waived.
questions.call: How should the court rule on the second motion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Grant it, because a personal-jurisdiction defense may be raised at any time before trial.
choices.rationale: Unlike subject-matter jurisdiction, personal jurisdiction is a waivable defense and must be included in the first Rule 12 response when Rule 12(g) requires consolidation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Deny it, because the defendant waived the defense by omitting it from the first Rule 12 motion.
choices.rationale: Rule 12(g)(2) and Rule 12(h)(1) require a defendant to consolidate the defenses listed in Rule 12(b)(2)-(5) in the first Rule 12 motion. Omission waives the personal-jurisdiction defense.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Grant it, because the defendant had not yet filed a responsive pleading when the second motion was made.
choices.rationale: The filing of an answer is not the relevant event. The defendant already made a Rule 12 motion and was required to include the personal-jurisdiction defense in it.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Deny it only if the defendant had actual notice of the personal-jurisdiction defect when filing the first motion.
choices.rationale: Waiver under Rules 12(g) and 12(h) does not depend on proof that the defendant subjectively recognized the omitted defense.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a defendant waives lack of personal jurisdiction by omitting it from an initial Rule 12 motion.
explanation.governingRule: A party that makes a Rule 12 motion must consolidate available defenses under Rule 12(b)(2)-(5). A defense of lack of personal jurisdiction is waived if omitted from that motion.
explanation.application: The defendant first moved under Rule 12(b)(6) without asserting lack of personal jurisdiction. The later Rule 12(b)(2) motion is therefore barred by the consolidation and waiver rules.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming that any defense may be added before the answer. The first Rule 12 motion triggers the consolidation requirement.
explanation.memoryAid: PJ, venue, process, and service: raise them in the first Rule 12 response or lose them.

authoring.contentWarning: Internally audited for one-best-answer structure and legal coherence, but a qualified attorney must verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

qualityAudit.secondPassNotes: Second pass made clear that the first motion preceded the answer and that the omitted defense was personal jurisdiction, one of the waivable Rule 12(b)(2)-(5) defenses.

questions.subject: Civil Procedure
questions.subtopic: Preliminary injunctions and temporary restraining orders - notice requirement
questions.stem: A homeowner filed a federal action challenging a city's planned demolition of her house. The city announced that demolition would begin the next morning. The homeowner submitted a verified complaint describing immediate and irreparable harm and asked the court to issue a preliminary injunction without notifying the city. Her attorney filed a written certification describing unsuccessful efforts to reach the city attorney and explaining why notice could not be completed before demolition.
questions.call: May the court issue the requested preliminary injunction without notice to the city?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because the verified complaint establishes a threat of immediate and irreparable injury.
choices.rationale: Immediate injury may support an ex parte temporary restraining order if Rule 65(b) is satisfied, but a preliminary injunction may issue only on notice to the adverse party.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because the attorney documented unsuccessful efforts to provide notice.
choices.rationale: The certification is relevant to an ex parte TRO. It does not eliminate Rule 65(a)(1)'s notice requirement for a preliminary injunction.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because a preliminary injunction may issue only after notice, although an ex parte temporary restraining order may be available.
choices.rationale: Rule 65(a)(1) categorically requires notice before a preliminary injunction. The facts may satisfy the separate requirements for an ex parte TRO.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because federal courts may never grant emergency injunctive relief before the adverse party appears.
choices.rationale: Rule 65(b) permits a narrowly limited ex parte TRO when the specified factual showing and attorney certification are made.

explanation.testedIssue: The distinction between the notice required for a preliminary injunction and the circumstances permitting an ex parte TRO.
explanation.governingRule: A preliminary injunction may issue only on notice to the adverse party. A court may issue a temporary restraining order without notice only if specific facts show immediate and irreparable injury and counsel certifies efforts to give notice and reasons notice should not be required.
explanation.application: The homeowner has facts potentially supporting an ex parte TRO, but the court cannot issue the requested preliminary injunction until the city receives notice.
explanation.commonTrap: Treating all emergency injunctions as interchangeable. Rule 65 distinguishes a noticed preliminary injunction from a short-lived ex parte TRO.
explanation.memoryAid: PI requires notice; an ex parte TRO requires emergency facts plus counsel's certification.

authoring.contentWarning: Internally audited for one-best-answer structure and legal coherence, but a qualified attorney must verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

qualityAudit.secondPassNotes: Second pass separated the relief requested from relief potentially available and included both Rule 65(b) prerequisites so the question does not turn on a missing TRO fact.

questions.subject: Civil Procedure
questions.subtopic: Discovery - physical examination under Rule 35
questions.stem: In a federal personal-injury action, the plaintiff alleged that a collision caused a permanent spinal injury. The defendant served a notice directing the plaintiff to appear for an examination by an orthopedic physician selected by the defendant. The notice specified the time, place, manner, conditions, and scope of the examination. The defendant did not move for a court order. The plaintiff refused to attend, and the defendant moved to compel compliance based solely on the notice.
questions.call: Should the court compel the plaintiff to attend the examination?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because the plaintiff placed her physical condition in controversy by seeking damages for a permanent injury.
choices.rationale: Putting the condition in controversy is necessary but not sufficient. Rule 35 requires a court order made on motion for good cause and with notice to the person and all parties.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because a party may obtain any nonprivileged discovery proportional to the needs of the case by serving notice.
choices.rationale: The general scope of discovery does not displace Rule 35's specific requirement of a court order for a physical or mental examination.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because a party may be examined only by a physician selected jointly by the parties.
choices.rationale: Rule 35 does not require joint selection. The court order may specify a suitably licensed or certified examiner.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because a physical examination requires a court order entered on motion for good cause.
choices.rationale: A Rule 35 examination is not self-executing through notice alone. The defendant must obtain an order after showing that the condition is in controversy and good cause exists.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether notice alone is sufficient to require a party to undergo a physical examination under Rule 35.
explanation.governingRule: A court may order a party whose physical or mental condition is in controversy to submit to an examination only on motion for good cause and notice to the person and all parties. The order must specify the examination's details.
explanation.application: Although the plaintiff's spinal condition is in controversy and the notice contains detailed terms, the defendant never obtained the required court order.
explanation.commonTrap: Analogizing a Rule 35 examination to a deposition, which generally may be scheduled by notice without first obtaining an order.
explanation.memoryAid: Rule 35 exam: condition in controversy, good cause, and a court order.

authoring.contentWarning: Internally audited for one-best-answer structure and legal coherence, but a qualified attorney must verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

qualityAudit.secondPassNotes: Second pass removed any dispute about whether the condition was in controversy and made the missing court order the single dispositive defect.

questions.subject: Civil Procedure
questions.subtopic: Final judgment rule - unresolved claims and Rule 54(b)
questions.stem: A plaintiff asserted separate breach-of-contract and fraud claims against a defendant in federal district court. The court granted summary judgment to the defendant on the contract claim but left the fraud claim for trial. The court did not direct entry of judgment on the contract claim and did not determine that there was no just reason for delay. The plaintiff immediately filed a notice of appeal from the ruling on the contract claim.
questions.call: Should the court of appeals hear the appeal?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because the order did not resolve all claims and the district court made no Rule 54(b) certification.
choices.rationale: An order disposing of fewer than all claims ordinarily is not final. Without an express direction for entry of judgment and a determination that there is no just reason for delay, the ruling remains interlocutory.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because an order granting summary judgment can never be appealed before trial on the remaining claims.
choices.rationale: A partial summary judgment may be immediately appealable if properly certified under Rule 54(b), and other exceptions may also permit interlocutory review.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because summary judgment conclusively resolved the contract claim.
choices.rationale: Conclusive resolution of one claim is not enough when another claim remains and there is no Rule 54(b) certification.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, because the contract and fraud claims are legally distinct causes of action.
choices.rationale: The distinctness of the claims permits potential Rule 54(b) certification but does not itself create a final, appealable judgment.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a ruling that resolves one of several claims is immediately appealable without Rule 54(b) certification.
explanation.governingRule: Under the final-judgment rule, an appeal ordinarily lies only after all claims as to all parties are resolved. In a multiclaim action, the district court may direct entry of final judgment on fewer than all claims only by expressly determining that there is no just reason for delay.
explanation.application: The fraud claim remains pending, and the district court made neither of the findings required by Rule 54(b). The summary-judgment order is therefore not yet final.
explanation.commonTrap: Equating a final decision on one claim with a final judgment in the whole action.
explanation.memoryAid: One unresolved claim means no appeal unless Rule 54(b) or another exception applies.

authoring.contentWarning: Internally audited for one-best-answer structure and legal coherence, but a qualified attorney must verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

qualityAudit.secondPassNotes: Second pass specified that the claims are separate but that no Rule 54(b) direction or no-just-reason finding was made.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.subtopic: State sovereign immunity - prospective relief against a state officer
questions.stem: A state statute required the director of a state licensing agency to revoke the license of any business that published criticism of the governor. A newspaper company whose license was about to be revoked filed an action in federal district court against the director in her official capacity. The complaint alleged that the statute violated the First Amendment and sought only an injunction preventing the director from enforcing the statute in the future. The state had not consented to suit.
questions.call: Is the action barred by state sovereign immunity?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because a suit against a state officer in her official capacity is always treated as a suit against the state.
choices.rationale: Official-capacity suits for retrospective damages generally implicate state immunity, but Ex parte Young permits suits against state officers for prospective relief from ongoing violations of federal law.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because the company seeks prospective relief against a state officer for an alleged ongoing violation of federal law.
choices.rationale: The Ex parte Young doctrine allows a federal court to enjoin a state officer from prospectively enforcing an allegedly unconstitutional state law.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because Congress has not abrogated state immunity for this particular action.
choices.rationale: Congressional abrogation is not required when the plaintiff invokes the Ex parte Young exception and seeks prospective relief against a responsible state officer.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, but only because the First Amendment automatically eliminates sovereign immunity for every form of relief.
choices.rationale: The type of relief matters. The doctrine permits prospective injunctive relief; it does not automatically authorize retrospective monetary relief against the state.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a federal court may hear an official-capacity action seeking prospective relief from enforcement of an allegedly unconstitutional state law.
explanation.governingRule: State sovereign immunity does not bar an action against a state officer in her official capacity for prospective injunctive relief designed to end an ongoing violation of federal law.
explanation.application: The company seeks no damages or payment from the state treasury. It asks only that the director be enjoined from future enforcement of the challenged statute.
explanation.commonTrap: Treating every official-capacity suit as barred without distinguishing prospective relief from retrospective relief.
explanation.memoryAid: Ex parte Young: sue the responsible officer to stop an ongoing federal-law violation.

authoring.contentWarning: Internally audited for one-best-answer structure and legal coherence, but a qualified attorney must verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

qualityAudit.secondPassNotes: Second pass limited the requested remedy to prospective injunctive relief and identified the officer charged with enforcement, avoiding ambiguity about retrospective treasury relief.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.subtopic: Congressional commerce power - aggregation of intrastate economic activity
questions.stem: Congress enacted a comprehensive statute regulating the national market for a fungible grain that is bought and sold throughout the United States. To stabilize supply and prices, the statute limited the amount of the grain that each farm could produce. A farmer knowingly exceeded the limit but used all of the excess grain to feed animals on his own farm and neither sold nor transported it. The federal government imposed a civil penalty on the farmer under the statute.
questions.call: Is the penalty constitutional?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because Congress may regulate only goods that actually cross a state line.
choices.rationale: Congress may regulate intrastate economic activity when the activity, considered in the aggregate, has a substantial effect on interstate commerce.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because production for personal consumption is noneconomic activity beyond the commerce power.
choices.rationale: Producing a market commodity is economic activity. Home consumption can affect the interstate market by reducing demand for grain purchased in commerce.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because Congress may regulate the farmer's production as part of a comprehensive regulation of an interstate market.
choices.rationale: The farmer's production is economic and, when aggregated with similar production, could substantially affect supply and demand in the interstate grain market.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but only if the government proves that this farmer's excess grain alone changed interstate prices.
choices.rationale: Congress may consider the cumulative effect of the regulated class of economic activity; it need not prove a substantial interstate effect from one farmer's conduct in isolation.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether Congress may reach intrastate production for personal use as part of a comprehensive economic regulation.
explanation.governingRule: Congress may regulate intrastate economic activity that, in the aggregate, substantially affects interstate commerce, particularly when regulation is an essential part of a broader scheme governing an interstate market.
explanation.application: The grain is a fungible market commodity. Production for on-farm use substitutes for purchases from the national market, and aggregate excess production could undermine Congress's supply controls.
explanation.commonTrap: Demanding proof that the individual defendant's conduct, standing alone, substantially affected interstate commerce.
explanation.memoryAid: Economic activity can be aggregated; one person's small effect is not the measure.

authoring.contentWarning: Internally audited for one-best-answer structure and legal coherence, but a qualified attorney must verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

qualityAudit.secondPassNotes: Second pass described a comprehensive regulation of a fungible interstate commodity and avoided relying on noneconomic conduct, which would trigger a different Commerce Clause analysis.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.subtopic: Presidential power - refusal to spend funds that Congress directed be disbursed
questions.stem: Congress enacted a statute directing the Secretary of Transportation to distribute a specified sum to every city that met stated eligibility criteria. The statute appropriated the money and gave the executive branch no discretion to reduce an eligible city's award. All eligible cities were identified. The President, believing that immediate distribution would worsen inflation, ordered the Secretary to withhold half of the appropriated funds. No other statute authorized the withholding.
questions.call: Is the President's order constitutional?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because the President has inherent authority to control the timing and amount of all federal expenditures.
choices.rationale: The President must execute duly enacted spending laws and has no general power to cancel or reduce mandatory appropriations based solely on policy disagreement.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because inflation control is an area in which presidential policy judgments are not judicially reviewable.
choices.rationale: The issue is whether the President complied with a statutory command, a question courts can adjudicate. A policy justification does not create discretion the statute withheld.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because the President may reduce an appropriation only by exercising a line-item veto.
choices.rationale: The Constitution does not grant the President a line-item veto, and the Supreme Court has held a statutory line-item veto unconstitutional. That is not the source of the defect here.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because the President may not disregard Congress's mandatory direction to distribute the appropriated funds.
choices.rationale: When Congress has lawfully required disbursement and left no executive discretion, the President must faithfully execute that command rather than substitute a contrary spending policy.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether the President may impound funds when a valid statute requires their distribution and leaves no executive discretion.
explanation.governingRule: The President must faithfully execute valid federal statutes and may not frustrate a clear congressional command to spend appropriated funds when Congress has provided no discretion to withhold them.
explanation.application: The statute identifies the recipients and amounts and directs distribution. The President's inflation concern does not authorize withholding contrary to the statutory command.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming that control over executive agencies includes a general power to revise the substance of spending legislation.
explanation.memoryAid: Execution is not amendment: the President cannot rewrite a mandatory appropriation.

authorityNotes: U.S. Const. art. II, § 3 (Take Care Clause)
Train v. City of New York, 420 U.S. 35 (1975)
Clinton v. City of New York, 524 U.S. 417 (1998)

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.subtopic: Equal protection - state alienage classification and political-function exception
questions.stem: A state operated several public hospitals. A state statute limited all permanent nursing positions at those hospitals to United States citizens. A lawful permanent resident who had lived in the state for 12 years, held every required professional license, and was otherwise qualified was denied a nursing position solely because she was not a citizen. She challenged the statute under the Equal Protection Clause.
questions.call: Which standard should a court apply to the citizenship restriction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Strict scrutiny, because nursing is not a position intimately related to democratic self-government.
choices.rationale: State alienage classifications ordinarily receive strict scrutiny. The political-function exception is narrow and does not cover ordinary professional nursing duties in a public hospital.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Intermediate scrutiny, because citizenship classifications are treated in the same manner as sex classifications.
choices.rationale: State classifications based on lawful alienage generally trigger strict scrutiny, not intermediate scrutiny.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Rational basis review, because every employee of a state institution performs a governmental function.
choices.rationale: The political-function exception does not encompass every public employee. It covers positions involving discretionary participation in the formulation or execution of public policy.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rational basis review, because lawful permanent residents are not protected by the Equal Protection Clause.
choices.rationale: Lawful permanent residents are persons protected by the Equal Protection Clause, and state alienage classifications generally receive heightened review.

explanation.testedIssue: The level of scrutiny for a state law excluding lawful permanent residents from public employment.
explanation.governingRule: State alienage classifications generally are subject to strict scrutiny, except for positions intimately related to the process of democratic self-government, for which rational basis review applies.
explanation.application: Hospital nurses provide professional medical care but do not formulate or execute sovereign policy. The narrow political-function exception therefore does not apply.
explanation.commonTrap: Treating the fact that the employer is the state as enough to invoke the political-function exception.
explanation.memoryAid: State alienage rule: strict scrutiny, unless the job is genuinely part of democratic governance.

authoring.contentWarning: Internally audited for one-best-answer structure and legal coherence, but a qualified attorney must verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

qualityAudit.secondPassNotes: Second pass selected a licensed clinical position with no policy-making authority, avoiding uncertainty about jobs such as police officers, teachers, or notaries that have generated political-function litigation.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.subtopic: Freedom of expression - licensing scheme with unbridled discretion
questions.stem: A city ordinance required a person to obtain a permit before distributing leaflets in any city park. The parks director could deny a permit whenever, in the director's judgment, the proposed distribution would be “inconsistent with the public welfare.” The ordinance supplied no additional standards, imposed no deadline for a decision, and provided no prompt judicial review. An advocacy group that wished to distribute political leaflets brought a facial challenge before applying for a permit.
questions.call: Is the ordinance constitutional?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because the group has not yet applied for and been denied a permit.
choices.rationale: A speaker may bring a facial challenge to a licensing scheme that vests unbridled discretion in an official without first applying for a permit.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because the ordinance gives the director unbounded discretion and lacks adequate procedural safeguards.
choices.rationale: A prior-licensing system for protected speech must contain narrow, objective standards and adequate procedures. The public-welfare standard, absence of deadlines, and lack of prompt review create a classic prior-restraint defect.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because a city may regulate all expressive activity occurring on government-owned property.
choices.rationale: Traditional public forums such as parks receive strong First Amendment protection. Government ownership does not permit unrestricted licensing discretion.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, but only because political leaflets may never be subject to a permit requirement.
choices.rationale: A content-neutral permit scheme may be valid if it uses objective standards, serves legitimate interests, and includes proper procedural safeguards. The defect is not the mere existence of a permit.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a speech-licensing ordinance is an unconstitutional prior restraint because it grants unbridled discretion and lacks safeguards.
explanation.governingRule: A licensing scheme affecting protected expression must employ narrow, objective, definite standards and adequate procedural safeguards. A person may facially challenge a scheme granting unbridled discretion without first seeking a license.
explanation.application: The director may deny a permit based on an undefined public-welfare judgment, with no decision deadline or prompt review. Those features invite censorship and indefinite delay.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming a permit requirement is automatically valid as a time, place, and manner regulation without examining the official's discretion and review procedures.
explanation.memoryAid: Speech permits need objective rules, prompt decisions, and prompt review.

authorityNotes: Shuttlesworth v. City of Birmingham, 394 U.S. 147 (1969)
City of Lakewood v. Plain Dealer Publishing Co., 486 U.S. 750 (1988)

questions.subject: Contracts
questions.subtopic: UCC acceptance by shipment - nonconforming goods sent as an accommodation
questions.stem: A furniture retailer sent a signed purchase order to a wholesaler requesting shipment of 100 blue chairs at $50 each, delivery by June 1. The wholesaler promptly shipped 100 red chairs. With the shipment, the wholesaler sent a conspicuous notice stating, “Blue chairs are unavailable. These red chairs are offered only as an accommodation.” The retailer rejected the shipment and sued the wholesaler for breach of contract.
questions.call: Is the wholesaler liable for breach?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because shipment of nonconforming goods simultaneously accepts the order and breaches the resulting contract.
choices.rationale: That is the usual rule when a seller ships nonconforming goods without seasonably identifying the shipment as an accommodation. The accompanying accommodation notice changes the result.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because a seller may make a counteroffer only before shipping any goods.
choices.rationale: Under UCC Article 2, a seasonably notified accommodation shipment operates as a counteroffer even though the goods are shipped.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because the seasonably notified accommodation shipment was a counteroffer rather than an acceptance.
choices.rationale: The purchase order invited acceptance by prompt shipment, but the wholesaler clearly and seasonably stated that the nonconforming shipment was offered only as an accommodation. No contract arose unless the retailer accepted that counteroffer.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because a contract for goods is not formed until the buyer accepts delivery.
choices.rationale: A sales contract can be formed before delivery through the parties' communications or by shipment. Buyer acceptance of delivered goods is not always required for formation.

explanation.testedIssue: The effect of shipping nonconforming goods with seasonable notice that the shipment is an accommodation.
explanation.governingRule: An order inviting prompt shipment may be accepted by shipping conforming or nonconforming goods. A shipment of nonconforming goods ordinarily is both acceptance and breach, unless the seller seasonably notifies the buyer that the shipment is offered only as an accommodation, in which case it is a counteroffer.
explanation.application: The wholesaler's notice accompanied the red chairs and expressly characterized them as an accommodation. The retailer rejected the counteroffer, so the shipment did not create a contract that the wholesaler breached.
explanation.commonTrap: Remembering that nonconforming shipment usually equals acceptance plus breach but overlooking the express accommodation notice.
explanation.memoryAid: Nonconforming shipment: acceptance and breach—unless clearly sent as an accommodation.

qualityAudit.secondPassNotes: Second pass placed the accommodation notice with the shipment, made it conspicuous and explicit, and avoided facts suggesting a prior contract independent of the purchase order.

questions.subject: Contracts
questions.subtopic: Statute of frauds - possibility of performance within one year
questions.stem: An engineering firm orally promised to pay a consultant $12,000 per month to supervise construction of a tunnel until the tunnel was completed. Both parties expected the project to take approximately 18 months, but the agreement did not establish a minimum duration, and completion within 10 months was physically possible. After three months, the firm discharged the consultant without cause and refused to pay amounts then due.
questions.call: Is the agreement unenforceable under the one-year provision of the Statute of Frauds?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because both parties expected performance to continue for longer than one year.
choices.rationale: The one-year provision turns on whether complete performance is possible within one year, not on the parties' expectations or probabilities.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because the consultant was to receive payments extending beyond one year if construction proceeded as expected.
choices.rationale: The expected payment schedule is not controlling when the contracted task could be fully completed within one year.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, but only because the firm partially performed by paying the consultant for three months.
choices.rationale: Partial performance generally does not remove a service contract from the one-year provision. The agreement is outside that provision because full performance was possible within one year.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because the tunnel could have been completed within one year of making the agreement.
choices.rationale: A contract is within the one-year provision only when its terms make performance within one year impossible. The possibility of completing the tunnel within 10 months keeps this agreement outside the provision.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether an oral agreement expected to last more than one year falls within the Statute of Frauds when complete performance within one year remains possible.
explanation.governingRule: The one-year provision applies only if, by its terms, the contract cannot possibly be fully performed within one year from formation. Expected or probable duration does not control.
explanation.application: The parties expected an 18-month project, but the agreement ended upon completion and the tunnel could have been completed within 10 months. Full performance within one year was therefore possible.
explanation.commonTrap: Using the most likely duration rather than asking whether any performance permitted by the contract could finish within one year.
explanation.memoryAid: One-year rule means impossible within a year—not merely unlikely.

authoring.contentWarning: Internally audited for one-best-answer structure and legal coherence, but a qualified attorney must verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

qualityAudit.secondPassNotes: Second pass stated that completion within 10 months was physically possible and that the contract imposed no minimum term, removing uncertainty about theoretical versus contractual possibility.

questions.subject: Contracts
questions.subtopic: Assignment of rights - effect of a prohibition that does not make assignments void
questions.stem: An architect completed all required services under a contract with a building owner and became entitled to a final payment of $60,000. The contract stated, “The architect shall not assign any right under this agreement.” It did not state that an assignment would be void or invalid. The architect nevertheless assigned the right to the final payment to a bank as security for a loan. The bank immediately notified the owner. The owner later paid the architect instead of the bank.
questions.call: May the bank recover the $60,000 from the owner?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because the assignment was effective even though making it may have breached the anti-assignment promise.
choices.rationale: A contractual prohibition ordinarily gives the obligor a claim for breach but does not invalidate an assignment unless the contract manifests an intent that an assignment be void. After notice, payment to the assignor does not discharge the owner's duty to the assignee.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, but only because the architect had fully performed before making the assignment.
choices.rationale: Full performance reduces concerns about materially changing the obligor's burden, but the central reason the assignment is effective is that the clause did not make assignments void. The same interpretive rule can apply before full performance, subject to other limits.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because any assignment made in violation of an anti-assignment clause is automatically ineffective.
choices.rationale: A mere promise not to assign ordinarily does not destroy the power to assign. Clear language declaring assignments void or invalid is generally required for that result.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because the owner discharged the payment obligation by paying the architect, the party named in the contract.
choices.rationale: Once the owner received notice of the effective assignment, payment to the architect was at the owner's risk and did not discharge the obligation owed to the bank.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a clause prohibiting assignment invalidates an assignment of an accrued payment right when the clause does not say that assignments are void.
explanation.governingRule: A term prohibiting assignment ordinarily creates a contractual duty not to assign but does not make an assignment ineffective unless a contrary intention is clearly manifested. Once the obligor receives notice of an effective assignment, payment to the assignor generally does not discharge the obligation.
explanation.application: The architect assigned an accrued right to money, the clause did not declare assignments void, and the owner had notice before paying the architect. The bank may enforce the payment right against the owner.
explanation.commonTrap: Conflating the assignor's power to assign with the assignor's contractual right to do so.
explanation.memoryAid: “Must not assign” may be a breach; “assignment is void” attacks effectiveness.

authoring.contentWarning: Internally audited for one-best-answer structure and legal coherence, but a qualified attorney must verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

qualityAudit.secondPassNotes: Second pass limited the assignment to an accrued monetary right, specified that the clause did not use voiding language, and established notice before payment.

questions.subject: Contracts
questions.subtopic: Liquidated damages - reasonable forecast of difficult-to-measure loss
questions.stem: A concert promoter hired a contractor to renovate an arena by May 1. At contracting, the parties knew that delay could require relocating events and losing uncertain ticket and concession revenue. They agreed that the contractor would pay $20,000 for each day of delay. Based on the information then available, both parties reasonably estimated likely delay losses at approximately $20,000 per day. The contractor finished five days late. The promoter's provable losses for those five days were $80,000.
questions.call: Is the promoter entitled to enforce the $100,000 liquidated-damages provision?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because liquidated damages are unenforceable whenever they exceed the loss ultimately proved at trial.
choices.rationale: A reasonable liquidated-damages amount is not invalid merely because actual loss later differs. Reasonableness is assessed in light of anticipated or actual loss and the difficulty of proof.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because the amount was a reasonable estimate of losses that were difficult to measure when the contract was made.
choices.rationale: The clause reasonably forecasted uncertain delay damages, and the $100,000 total is not grossly disproportionate to the $80,000 loss ultimately proved.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because a daily charge for delay is necessarily an unenforceable penalty.
choices.rationale: Per-day delay provisions are commonly enforceable when they reasonably estimate difficult-to-measure losses rather than punish breach.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, because parties may enforce any damages amount to which they expressly agree in writing.
choices.rationale: Freedom of contract does not permit enforcement of a penalty. The clause is enforceable because it is reasonable, not merely because it was written and agreed upon.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a stipulated delay amount is enforceable as liquidated damages rather than an unlawful penalty.
explanation.governingRule: A liquidated-damages clause is enforceable when actual loss is difficult to estimate and the stipulated amount is reasonable in light of anticipated or actual harm. A grossly disproportionate amount designed to compel performance is a penalty.
explanation.application: Delay losses were uncertain when the contract was formed, the parties reasonably estimated $20,000 per day, and the stipulated $100,000 is close to the $80,000 loss ultimately proved.
explanation.commonTrap: Evaluating the clause solely by hindsight or assuming every amount above actual damages is punitive.
explanation.memoryAid: Difficult to estimate plus reasonable amount equals valid liquidated damages.

authoring.contentWarning: Internally audited for one-best-answer structure and legal coherence, but a qualified attorney must verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

qualityAudit.secondPassNotes: Second pass narrowed the gap between stipulated and actual loss and expressly established the reasonableness of the contemporaneous estimate, reducing penalty ambiguity.

questions.subject: Contracts
questions.subtopic: Third-party beneficiaries - vesting and modification
questions.stem: An uncle promised a contractor that, in exchange for remodeling the uncle's office, the uncle would pay $30,000 directly to the contractor's daughter when she graduated from college. The contract expressly identified the daughter and the payment. The daughter did not know of the contract. Before she learned of it, relied on it, assented to it, or brought suit, the uncle and the contractor mutually agreed to rescind the payment provision. The daughter later graduated and learned of the original agreement.
questions.call: May the daughter enforce the original payment provision?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because an intended beneficiary's rights vest immediately when the contract is formed.
choices.rationale: An intended beneficiary may have enforceable rights, but the original parties generally retain power to modify or rescind until those rights vest through assent, suit, or material reliance.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because the daughter satisfied the stated condition by graduating from college.
choices.rationale: Graduation would have triggered payment under the original term, but the term was validly rescinded before the daughter's beneficiary rights vested.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because the contracting parties rescinded the provision before the daughter's rights vested.
choices.rationale: The daughter was an intended beneficiary, but she had neither assented, sued, nor materially relied before the parties rescinded. They therefore retained the power to eliminate the benefit.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because a person who furnishes no consideration can never enforce a contract made for that person's benefit.
choices.rationale: An intended third-party beneficiary need not furnish consideration. The daughter loses because her rights had not vested before rescission, not because she gave no consideration.

explanation.testedIssue: When an intended third-party beneficiary's rights become protected from modification or rescission by the original parties.
explanation.governingRule: Unless the contract provides otherwise, the promisor and promisee may modify or discharge an intended beneficiary's right until the beneficiary materially changes position in justifiable reliance, brings suit, or manifests assent at a party's request.
explanation.application: The daughter was specifically intended to receive payment, but she had no knowledge and took none of the actions that would vest her rights before the rescission.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming that identifying an intended beneficiary makes the benefit irrevocable from the instant of formation.
explanation.memoryAid: Beneficiary rights lock in with reliance, suit, or requested assent.

authoring.contentWarning: Internally audited for one-best-answer structure and legal coherence, but a qualified attorney must verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

qualityAudit.secondPassNotes: Second pass expressly negated each recognized vesting event and placed rescission before the daughter learned of the agreement.

questions.subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
questions.subtopic: Embezzlement - conversion of property lawfully possessed
questions.stem: An investor delivered $50,000 to a financial manager under an agreement requiring the manager to hold and invest the money in securities for the investor. The manager lawfully took possession and placed the money in a segregated client account. Several weeks later, the manager decided to use the money to pay personal gambling debts, withdrew it, and permanently converted it to his own use. The jurisdiction defines property crimes according to the common law.
questions.call: Of which crime is the manager most clearly guilty?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Larceny, because he intended to deprive the investor permanently of the money.
choices.rationale: Larceny requires a trespassory taking from another's possession. The manager obtained possession lawfully under the investment agreement before forming the intent to convert the money.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Larceny by trick, because the investor transferred possession but not title to the money.
choices.rationale: Larceny by trick requires fraud at the time possession is obtained. The manager lawfully received the money and formed the fraudulent intent only afterward.

choices.label: C
choices.text: False pretenses, because he obtained the money through a position of trust.
choices.rationale: False pretenses requires obtaining title through an intentional material misrepresentation. The investor did not transfer title to the manager, and no false representation induced the transfer.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Embezzlement, because he fraudulently converted property that was lawfully in his possession.
choices.rationale: The manager had lawful possession of the investor's money under the investment agreement and later intentionally converted it. Those facts satisfy common-law embezzlement.

explanation.testedIssue: The distinction between embezzlement and other common-law theft offenses.
explanation.governingRule: Embezzlement is the fraudulent conversion of property of another by a person who is in lawful possession of that property. Larceny requires a trespassory taking, and false pretenses requires obtaining title by fraud.
explanation.application: The manager lawfully possessed the money under the investment agreement and formed the intent to appropriate it only weeks later. His later conversion is embezzlement.
explanation.commonTrap: Focusing only on the intent to deprive. The decisive distinction is how and when the defendant obtained possession.
explanation.memoryAid: Lawful possession first, fraudulent conversion later: embezzlement.

authoring.contentWarning: Internally audited for one-best-answer structure and legal coherence, but a qualified attorney must verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

qualityAudit.secondPassNotes: Second pass used an independent financial manager with a segregated client account, fixed the intent after lawful possession, and stated that the jurisdiction uses common-law offense definitions.

questions.subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
questions.subtopic: Self-defense - initial aggressor who withdraws and communicates withdrawal
questions.stem: During an argument, a man deliberately shoved another person and raised his fists, intending to start a fistfight. The other person pulled a knife. The man immediately stepped backward, lowered his hands, and said, “I am done. I do not want to fight.” The other person advanced with the knife and threatened to kill him. Unable to retreat safely, the man reasonably believed that he was about to be stabbed and used deadly force to stop the attack. The jurisdiction follows the common-law rules of self-defense.
questions.call: May the man successfully claim self-defense?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because he clearly withdrew from the encounter and communicated that withdrawal before using deadly force.
choices.rationale: An initial aggressor can regain the right of self-defense by actually withdrawing and communicating the withdrawal. The other person then continued with a threatened deadly attack.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because a person who responds with a knife becomes the sole aggressor regardless of how the encounter began.
choices.rationale: Escalation can affect the aggressor analysis, but it does not automatically erase the first actor's aggressor status in every case. The man's clear withdrawal and communication are what restore his privilege here.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because an initial aggressor can never regain the right to use self-defense during the same encounter.
choices.rationale: An initial aggressor may regain the defense by withdrawing in good faith and effectively communicating that withdrawal.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because deadly force is never permissible in response to a knife attack that has not yet caused injury.
choices.rationale: A person need not wait to be injured. Deadly force may be used when the person reasonably believes it is necessary to prevent imminent death or serious bodily harm.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether an initial aggressor may regain the right to self-defense by withdrawing and communicating that withdrawal.
explanation.governingRule: An initial aggressor ordinarily cannot claim self-defense, but may regain the privilege by withdrawing in good faith and effectively communicating the withdrawal. Deadly force is then justified if reasonably necessary to prevent imminent death or serious bodily harm.
explanation.application: The man stepped back, disarmed his posture, expressly ended the confrontation, and faced an advancing knife attack with no safe retreat. His withdrawal restored the defense before he used deadly force.
explanation.commonTrap: Treating initial-aggressor status as permanent regardless of withdrawal or the other participant's subsequent conduct.
explanation.memoryAid: Aggressor can reset the encounter: withdraw and communicate.

authoring.contentWarning: Internally audited for one-best-answer structure and legal coherence, but a qualified attorney must verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

qualityAudit.secondPassNotes: Second pass added an explicit verbal and physical withdrawal, an imminent deadly threat, and no safe retreat so the result does not depend on jurisdictional retreat rules.

questions.subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
questions.subtopic: Conspiracy - withdrawal after the agreement but before substantive offenses
questions.stem: A woman and a man agreed to burglarize a warehouse the following night. The jurisdiction follows the common-law rule that conspiracy is complete upon agreement and requires no overt act. Several hours before the planned burglary, the woman told the man unequivocally that she was withdrawing and would not participate. She also gave the police enough information to prevent the crime. The police failed to act, and the man committed the burglary alone.
questions.call: Of which crimes may the woman be convicted?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The woman may not be convicted of either offense.
choices.rationale: Withdrawal does not undo a conspiracy that was already complete upon agreement. Thus she remains liable for conspiracy even though withdrawal can limit responsibility for later crimes.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The woman may be convicted of conspiracy, but not burglary.
choices.rationale: The conspiracy was complete when the parties agreed. The woman then took timely affirmative steps to withdraw and notify authorities, cutting off liability for the later burglary committed after withdrawal.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The woman may be convicted of burglary, but not conspiracy.
choices.rationale: The agreement established conspiracy under the stated common-law rule. Her effective withdrawal prevents liability for the later burglary rather than eliminating the conspiracy conviction.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The woman may be convicted of both conspiracy and burglary.
choices.rationale: Her timely communication to the man and report to police were sufficient to terminate responsibility for the later burglary, even though the police failed to prevent it.

explanation.testedIssue: The effect of withdrawal from a completed conspiracy on conspiracy liability and liability for later substantive offenses.
explanation.governingRule: At common law, conspiracy is complete upon agreement. Later withdrawal is not a defense to conspiracy, but timely affirmative withdrawal communicated to co-conspirators—and, when appropriate, efforts to thwart the crime—can terminate liability for subsequent offenses committed in furtherance of the conspiracy.
explanation.application: The woman remained guilty of conspiracy because the agreement was complete. Her unequivocal withdrawal and report to police occurred before the burglary and cut off derivative liability for that later crime.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming withdrawal either does everything or nothing. It does not erase conspiracy, but it can stop future co-conspirator liability.
explanation.memoryAid: Withdrawal looks forward, not backward.

authoring.contentWarning: Internally audited for one-best-answer structure and legal coherence, but a qualified attorney must verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

qualityAudit.secondPassNotes: Second pass expressly supplied the jurisdiction's no-overt-act rule, placed withdrawal before the burglary, and included both communication and police notification.

questions.subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
questions.subtopic: Fourth Amendment - consent by a co-occupant with common authority
questions.stem: Police officers went to an apartment shared by two roommates to investigate drug sales. One roommate answered the door. The other roommate was away and had not previously objected to a search. The roommate who answered told the officers that both tenants jointly used the kitchen and living room and consented to a search of those areas. In a kitchen cabinet used by both roommates, the officers found illegal drugs belonging to the absent roommate.
questions.call: Is the evidence admissible against the absent roommate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because one occupant can never consent to a search that may produce evidence against another occupant.
choices.rationale: A person with common authority over shared premises may consent to a search of those common areas, and the consent is valid against an absent co-occupant.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because the police needed a warrant once they suspected that the drugs belonged to the absent roommate.
choices.rationale: Valid third-party consent is an exception to the warrant requirement. Ownership of the seized item does not invalidate consent to search a shared location.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because the consenting roommate had common authority over the shared areas and cabinet.
choices.rationale: Joint access and control creates authority to consent to a search of common areas. The absent roommate assumed the risk that the co-occupant would permit entry.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but only if the consenting roommate also owned the drugs found in the cabinet.
choices.rationale: Authority to consent turns on joint access or control over the place searched, not ownership of the evidence discovered there.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a co-occupant may consent to a search of jointly used areas when another occupant is absent.
explanation.governingRule: A person who possesses common authority through mutual use and joint access or control may consent to a search of shared premises. That consent is generally valid against an absent co-occupant who has not contemporaneously objected.
explanation.application: The consenting roommate jointly used the kitchen, living room, and cabinet. The officers confined the search to those common areas while the other roommate was absent.
explanation.commonTrap: Focusing on who owned the contraband rather than who had authority over the place searched.
explanation.memoryAid: Consent follows common control of the space, not ownership of the evidence.

authoring.contentWarning: Internally audited for one-best-answer structure and legal coherence, but a qualified attorney must verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

qualityAudit.secondPassNotes: Second pass confined the search to undeniably shared areas and stated that the absent occupant had not made a contemporaneous objection.

questions.subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
questions.subtopic: Miranda - questioning by an undercover agent
questions.stem: A robbery suspect was arrested on an unrelated charge and placed in a jail cell. Before any formal robbery charge had been filed, police placed an undercover officer in the cell. The suspect believed the officer was another detainee. During a casual conversation initiated by the undercover officer, the suspect voluntarily described how he had committed the robbery. The officer gave no Miranda warnings and used no threats or promises.
questions.call: Is the suspect's statement admissible in a later robbery prosecution?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because any questioning of a person who is physically in custody requires Miranda warnings.
choices.rationale: Miranda addresses custodial interrogation in a police-dominated setting. An unsuspecting conversation with an undercover agent does not create the coercive pressures Miranda is designed to dispel.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because the undercover officer deliberately elicited the incriminating statement.
choices.rationale: Deliberate elicitation is relevant to the Sixth Amendment after adversarial proceedings begin. No robbery charge had been filed, and Miranda does not require warnings for undercover questioning of this kind.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, but only because the suspect was jailed on a charge unrelated to the robbery.
choices.rationale: The absence of formal robbery charges matters to the Sixth Amendment, but the Miranda result rests on the lack of a coercive, known police interrogation. Undercover questioning can fall outside Miranda even when the subject matter is related to custody.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, because questioning by an undercover officer whom the suspect believes to be a fellow detainee does not require Miranda warnings.
choices.rationale: Because the suspect did not know he was speaking to law enforcement, the exchange lacked the police-dominated coercive atmosphere that triggers Miranda protections. The statement was also voluntary.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether Miranda warnings are required before an undercover officer questions an incarcerated suspect who does not know he is speaking to police.
explanation.governingRule: Miranda warnings are required for custodial interrogation, but not for questioning by an undercover agent when the suspect is unaware that the questioner is a law-enforcement officer, because the coercive pressures underlying Miranda are absent. The statement must still be voluntary.
explanation.application: The suspect believed he was speaking casually with another detainee, no robbery charge had initiated the Sixth Amendment right, and no coercion made the statement involuntary.
explanation.commonTrap: Equating physical custody with Miranda interrogation without considering whether the suspect knows police are questioning him.
explanation.memoryAid: Undercover cellmate: custody exists, but Miranda coercion does not.

questions.subject: Evidence
questions.category: Hearsay and circumstances of its admissibility
questions.subtopic: Present sense impression - statement describing an event while observing it
questions.stem: In a negligence action arising from a collision, the plaintiff sought to prove that a blue sedan entered an intersection against a red light. A pedestrian standing at the intersection had sent a text message to a friend while watching the event: “A blue sedan is running the red light and heading north.” The pedestrian was unavailable at trial. The parties stipulated that the message was authentic and had been sent at the time shown on the phone.
questions.call: Is the text message admissible to prove how the sedan entered the intersection?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because it described the sedan's movement while the pedestrian was observing it.
choices.rationale: The pedestrian described the event while perceiving it. Rule 803(1) permits such a statement regardless of whether the declarant is available.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, but only if the pedestrian was startled by the sedan.
choices.rationale: A startling event and continued stress are requirements for an excited utterance, not for a present sense impression.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because the pedestrian is unavailable for cross-examination.
choices.rationale: The present-sense-impression exception applies regardless of the declarant's availability. In this civil case, the Confrontation Clause is not implicated.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because a written electronic message cannot qualify as a present sense impression.
choices.rationale: Rule 803(1) is not limited to oral statements. A contemporaneous text can qualify if properly authenticated and sufficiently contemporaneous.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a contemporaneous text describing an observed event qualifies as a present sense impression.
explanation.governingRule: A statement describing or explaining an event or condition is admissible as a present sense impression if made while or immediately after the declarant perceived it. The exception applies regardless of availability.
explanation.application: The pedestrian sent the authenticated text while watching the sedan run the light. Its content directly described the event as perceived.
explanation.commonTrap: Importing the excited-utterance requirement of a startling event into the separate present-sense-impression exception.
explanation.memoryAid: See it, say it now: present sense impression.

authoring.contentWarning: Internally audited for one-best-answer structure and legal coherence, but a qualified attorney must verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

qualityAudit.secondPassNotes: Second pass stipulated authentication and contemporaneous transmission so admissibility turns only on the hearsay exception.

questions.subject: Evidence
questions.category: Hearsay and circumstances of its admissibility
questions.subtopic: Former testimony - unavailable declarant and prior opportunity with similar motive
questions.stem: A pedestrian sued a bus company after being struck by one of its buses. After an eyewitness was diagnosed with a terminal illness, the parties took a videotaped deposition to preserve the witness's testimony for trial. The eyewitness stated that the bus entered the crosswalk after the traffic signal turned red. The bus company's lawyer fully cross-examined the witness about the signal, vantage point, and lighting conditions. The eyewitness died before trial, and the pedestrian offered the authenticated deposition against the bus company.
questions.call: Is the deposition testimony admissible?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because deposition testimony is hearsay whenever the witness cannot appear at trial.
choices.rationale: Former testimony given at a lawful deposition may fall within Rule 804(b)(1) when the declarant is unavailable and the opposing party had an opportunity and similar motive to examine the witness.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because the witness is unavailable and the bus company previously had an opportunity and similar motive to examine the witness.
choices.rationale: Death makes the eyewitness unavailable. The bus company cross-examined the eyewitness on the same factual issue and had a similar motive to undermine the testimony.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because every statement made under oath is excluded from the definition of hearsay.
choices.rationale: An oath alone does not make a statement nonhearsay. The testimony is admissible under an exception requiring unavailability, opportunity, and similar motive.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because the pedestrian, rather than the bus company, originally noticed the deposition.
choices.rationale: Who noticed the deposition is not dispositive. The key is that the party against whom the testimony is now offered had an opportunity and similar motive to examine the witness.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether testimony from a prior deposition is admissible when the declarant dies before trial.
explanation.governingRule: Former testimony is admissible if the declarant is unavailable, the testimony was given at a trial, hearing, or lawful deposition, and the party against whom it is offered had an opportunity and similar motive to develop the testimony by examination.
explanation.application: The witness died, the testimony came from a lawful deposition, and the bus company conducted extensive cross-examination on the same disputed facts.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming that testimony is admissible merely because it was sworn, or inadmissible merely because it came from discovery.
explanation.memoryAid: Former testimony needs unavailable declarant plus prior opportunity and similar motive.
